data.title: Chinese Yuan And Australian Dollar Reach New 2025 Highs
data.description:
Overview

With a US-Japanese deal in hand, and the prospects of an extended tariff truce between the US and China, many perceive some tail risks associated with the US foreign economic policy have diminished. This has encouraged the animal spirits and helped drive equity prices higher. The dollar is mixed. The uptick in Australia's PMI and cautionary comments from the governor of the Reserve Bank of Australia have helped lift the Australian dollar to new highs for the year and put it atop the G10 performers today. On the other hand, a disappointing UK PMI pushed sterling down around 0.25% today and is the laggard among the G10 currencies. Emerging market currencies are mixed. Thai-Cambodia clashes weigh on the baht. The PBOC set the dollar's fix at a new low since last November, and the greenback recorded a new low for the year against the offshore yuan (below CNH7.15) before recovering.

Australia, South Korea, and India are exceptions in the rally in Asia, which saw the Nikkei extend yesterday's rally with another 1.6% gain today. The MSCI Asia Pacific Index was up about 0.8% today. Europe's Stoxx 600 gapped slightly higher today and is up about 0.65% after rising nearly 1.1% yesterday. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq futures are trading firmly. Bond markets are under pressure. In Europe, benchmark 10-year rates are mostly about three basis points higher, though the 10-year Gilt yield is nearly flat. While the 10-eyar JGB yields edged up to almost 1.60%, the yields on the 30- and 40-year JGBs soften a bit. The 10-year US Treasury yield is up a little more than a basis point to approach 4.40%. Gold reversed lower after reaching almost $3439 yesterday and settled slightly above $3387. It has been sold to nearly $3362 today. The week's low, set Monday, was around $3345. September WTI is recovering from yesterday's dip below $65 and is trying to find footing above $66 today. The 200-day moving average is slightly above $66.

USD: The Dollar Index overshot the (61.8%) retracement of this month's gain, seen near 97.35 to fall to a little below 97.20. It slipped to 97.10 today but is hovering around 97.40 in late European morning turnover. The US reports weekly jobless claims, which have fallen for the past five weeks, the longest decline since Aug-Sept 2022. However, next week's non-farm payroll report has more heft, and the early call is for job growth to have slowed (~115k vs. 147k in June) and for the unemployment rate to have risen (4.2% vs. 4.1%). Also, on tap today are new home sales, which are expected to stabilize after a heady 13.7% decline in May. The preliminary PMI is also due. The manufacturing PMI may slip for the first time since March. The services PMI may pare the 0.8% fall in June. The composite (output) PMI is seen easing for the second consecutive month.

Euro: The euro held above $1.0710 yesterday and ratcheted up to$1.1775, a 12-day high, helped by creeping optimism that a trade deal will be struck. It reached $1.1780 today before pulling back to almost $1.1745. The high set earlier this month was closer to $1.1830, which had not been seen since September 2021. The flash July PMI was slightly firmer sequentially, but the manufacturing sector continues to contract, albeit slowly (49.8 vs. 49.5). Yet the PMI has risen for the seventh consecutive month and has not been above 50 since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in early 2022. Services activity is improving. The 51.2 reading (50.5 in June) is the best since January. The composite edged higher for the second consecutive month, and at 51.0 (50.6 in June) matched the highest level since May 2024. It has held above 50 this year after 2024 finished at 49.6. The German economy appears to be struggling to sustain the upside momentum. The composite PMI slipped slightly (50.3 vs. 50.4 in June) after dipping to 48.5 in May, though the manufacturing and services PMI ticked up. The French PMIs, the manufacturing, services, and composite PMI, remain below 50. The French composite PMI has not been above 50 since last August (which was a one-month wonder). It stands at 49.6 (49.2 in June). The ECB meeting is as much of forgone conclusion as these things get. There is practically no chance of a change in policy with the deposit rate at 2%. ECB President Lagarde is unlikely to provide much forward guidance, but the market sees the central bank on hold until at least the end of the December meeting. The swaps market has about a 50% chance of a September cut and about a 65% chance of a cut at the end of the October meeting. Lastly, the EU-China summit today may be important in the bigger picture but does not seem impactful today.

CNY: Many, if not most observers, share our understanding that the PBOC's daily dollar fix is the chief way that Beijing manages the exchange rate. With Chinese exporters earning dollar revenue in a weakening dollar environment, it is not surprising that they are repatriating earnings and buying yuan. This leaves the banks as sellers of yuan and buyers of dollars. Yet, when some observers read media reports of state-owned banks buying dollars, they conclude it is "stealth intervention." We recognize two developments: First, the PBOC has adjusted the dollar's daily reference by a larger magnitude than at the start of the year. Second, since mid-April, the PBOC has been fairly steadily lowering the dollar's fix, and it is now the lowest since last November. Today's reference rate was set at CNY7.1385 (CNY7.1414 yesterday). The dollar was sold to a new low for the year against the offshore yuan near CNH7.1440 today before recovering to new session highs near CNH7.1545. The five-day moving average is slipping through the 20-day moving average. Note that the CNH7.1460 area corresponds to the (61.8%) retracement of the dollar's rally from last September's low (~CNH6.97) to April's high (~CNH7.43).

JPY: The dollar slipped in early North American turnover yesterday to almost JPY146.10. It eased below the 20-day moving average (~JPY146.20) for the first time in a couple of weeks. The mild follow-through dollar selling today met the (50%) retracement target of the rally from the July 1 low seen slightly below JPY145.95. The (61.8%) retracement is near JPY145.15. While many have focused on the trade agreement that brought down the reciprocal tariff and auto tariff to 15% from 25%, the agreement of $550 bln in direct investment is also significant. The details have not been published but consider that Japanese direct investment in the US tends to average $30-$40 bln a year. That includes retained earnings, while the new deal appears to envisage greenfield investment. And note that as of the end of 2024, Japan's stock of direct investment in the US was worth around $755 bln. The FDI commitment is around 18% of Japan's GDP. Meanwhile, the preliminary July composite PMI was steady at 51.5. The high for the year was seen in February at 52.0. The composite PMI was at 50.5 at the end of 2024. The manufacturing PMI rose above 50 in June for the first time since June 2024 but fell back below it in July (48.8). The services PMI jumped to 53.5 from 51.7. Its peak was also in February (53.7). Lastly, note that the swaps market is feeling more confident of a rate hike late this year. A little more than two weeks ago, the swaps market had about 10 bp of tightening discounted. It now stands at 21 bp, the most since early April.

GBP: Sterling rose for the third consecutive session yesterday and briefly traded to almost $1.3585. It reached the (50%) retracement target of the decline from the July 1 high. That retracement, near $1.3575, is above the 20-day moving average (~$1.3570). It has been turned back from almost $1.3590 today. Initial support is seen near $1.3515-20. The preliminary July composite PMI fell for the first time in three months to 51.0 from 52.0, which is the year's high. The manufacturing PMI rose for the fourth consecutive month to 48.2 (from 47.7). It has not been above 50 since last September. The services PMI eased to 51.2 (from 52.8). One might not know it by looking at the PMI, but the UK monthly GDP contracted in April and May, and after growing by 0.7% in Q1, the median economists forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for a 0.1% quarter-over-quarter expansion in Q2.

CAD: The US dollar recovered yesterday after it was initially sold to almost CAD1.3575. The year's low was set in mid-June near CAD1.3540 and this month's low was about CAD1.3555. On the bounce, the greenback reached around CAD1.3635, but it finished little changed around CAD1.3600. Options for around $380 mln expire there today. The CAD1.3650 area is the (38.2%) retracement objective of the US dollar's pullback from last week's high (~CAD1.3775). The 20-day moving average is slightly higher (~CAD1.3660) and the (50%) retracement is about CAD1.3675. Canada reports May retail sales today and StatsCan warns of the largest decline since the 1.2% decline in May 2024. Excluding autos, it may be the third consecutive decline, a streak not seen since the early days of the pandemic. Still, with the central bank having front-loaded rate cuts, there is little chance of a cut next week when the Bank of Canada meets (July 30).

AUD: The Australian dollar reached a new high for the year today near $0.6625. Recall that last Wednesday, the Aussie recorded the month's low near $0.6455. It settled firmly yesterday, slightly above $0.6600. The daily momentum indicators are mixed and there appears to be little resistance ahead of the $0.6700-$0.6715 area. Two developments have helped support the Aussie today. First, Australia's preliminary composite July PMI rose to a new high for the year 53.6 (vs. 51.6 in June). It is a new cyclical high. The manufacturing PMI quickened (51.6 vs. 50.6) to snap the deterioration of the past three months. The services PMI also stands at a new high for the year (53.8 vs 51.8). Second, central bank Governor Bullock seemed to signal caution with her emphasis on the “measured and gradual" approach to the easing cycle. Still, the market has little doubt that the Reserve Bank of Australia will cut rates at next month's meeting and deliver at least one more cut in Q4.

MXN: Mexico reports CPI for the first half of July. Economists in Bloomberg's survey expected the third consecutive year-over-year decline in the biweekly reading and the first sub-4% reading since the second half of April. In fact, if the median forecast in the survey is accurate (3.61%), it would be the lowest since the end of January. The overnight target rate is 8.0%, and the swaps market anticipates a quarter-point rate cut before the end of the year. For the sixth consecutive session, the dollar recorded a lower high against the peso. Yesterday, it stalled in front of MXN18.6854. The low, slightly above MXN18.5250, is a new low for the year. It is in an exceptionally narrow range today (~MXN18.53-MXN18.5525). There may be psychological support around MXN18.50, but we suspect the MXN18.35-MXN18.40 area is a reasonable target. Latam currencies were the top three performers among emerging market currencies yesterday (BRL +0.85%, COP +0.75%, and MXN + 0.60%).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Retire In Splendor And Massive 16% Yields: TriplePoint Venture
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

I spent four years as a bus driver for a large private school. In the morning, I would pick up students and carefully transport them to school. At noon, I would fill my bus with 72, four and five-year-olds and transport them safely to their homes. At the end of the school day, I would fill my bus up again with older students and drop them off as well. As each student got onto my bus, I would mark their attendance to properly account for when they hopped on and when they got off.

At the beginning of the year, I wouldn't know the students' names because they were new to me – unless they had ridden my bus the prior year. As the school year went on, I would quickly learn every student's name, but the speed at which I learned their name was often inversely proportional to their behavior. The better-behaved the child was, the longer it would take me to memorize their name, because I would have minimal opportunities to speak to them.

When it comes to a portfolio with 42 different holdings, at times, it can be the holdings that have the most drama around them that people remember the most. Interestingly, the High Dividend Opportunities' Model Portfolio has over 100 different holdings. Yet, if you were to ask our public readers what our holdings were, they would likely remember the most dramatic ones and not all of the quiet and steady performers in the background.

Today, I want to look at one of those more dramatic holdings and review its current risk/reward profile.

Let's dive in!

Coin Toss, But Happy With Both Outcomes

TriplePoint Venture Growth (NYSE:TPVG), yielding 16.6%* is a BDC (Business Development Company) that ran into credit issues. TPVG saw its assets decline from $978.8 million at the end of 2023 to $763 million at the end of 2024. Net investment income declined with it. Source

Note: This is an update on TPVG, and prior articles covering it in detail have been written; we encourage reading them as well for the deepest understanding of our investment outlook and theory.

A smaller portfolio produces less. While realized losses account for some of the decline, TPVG's deleveraging was the greatest driver, with leverage declining from 1.76x debt/equity to 1.10x.

During times of credit stress, deleveraging to maximize liquidity and ensure the company has flexibility on its balance sheet is essential. However, carrying less leverage directly translates to lower earnings.

1.76x is too high, but 1.10x is too low. TPVG's target is 1.3-1.4x.

In Q1, TPVG's NII (Net Investment Income) came in at $0.27/share, which is below its $0.30 dividend. To cover its dividend, TPVG will need to leverage up. In the Q1 earnings call, management noted that they were at the bottom end of their guidance range for new investments at $27 million. However, they noted that at the time of the earnings call, they had already funded $50 million in new investments and expected additional fundings later in the quarter to help make up for the shortfall in Q1.

This makes for a very interesting risk/reward going into Q2 earnings. If TPVG is able to accelerate its deployment of capital, we'll see a good increase in NII, and achieving dividend coverage could be a catalyst to bring TPVG's share price closer to NAV ($8.62 as of Q1). The market is skeptical, and we can't blame it, given how poorly the past two years have gone. We've been a bit skeptical since TPVG's dividend cut, recently noting:

"We still have an asterisk by the dividend and can see the potential of a cut to around $0.25/quarter as the point that is unquestionably sustainable. Would we be willing to buy TPVG today to receive $1/year in dividends, absolutely."

We've kept our "buy under" price, available to High Dividend Opportunities members, at a level where we would be okay with a $0.25 dividend. Plan for the worst, and if we collect more income, we aren't going to complain!

We see TPVG's earnings going one of two ways. The good scenario is that TPVG continues to increase its deployment of capital and NII rises, getting closer to covering the dividend, or actually covering it. This is something we think the market would respond favorably to, and we could see the discount to book value shrink. It's going to take some time for the market to forgive TPVG and price it at a premium again, but it could rise to an 8-10% discount. And of course, if the $0.30/quarter dividend is sustainable, the 16%+ yield is a great return on its own.

The risk is that TPVG decides it can't leverage up. Maybe a new credit issue surfaces in its portfolio, or maybe it isn't finding attractive opportunities to invest in, and the portfolio fails to grow. In that case, we would expect that the dividend would be cut to $0.25/quarter. That is the level we believed was sustainable when they first cut in Q3 of 2024. It is the easy bogey that they can make with a high degree of certainty. If that happens, the price probably would decline at first. However, nearly a year ago, we wrote:

"We are going to assume that TPVG's dividend will eventually be $0.25/quarter and going forward, we are only going to be willing to pay a price that we would happily pay if TPVG were paying only $1/year. To the extent that management is capable of providing better results than that, it's always nice to be paid more than you expect. Most of our BDCs have a yield of around 10%, TPVG is going in the penalty box and I want at least 14%. The price indicated by a $1 dividend at 14% yield is $7.15."

We were okay buying TPVG in the low $7s then if it was paying $0.25/quarter, and we are okay buying TPVG in the low $7s today even if you came from the future and told me that they will be paying $0.25/quarter.

So, while the price will probably react to a dividend cut if it happens, TPVG is already priced at a point we are willing to pay if the bad scenario happens. Meanwhile, the good scenario is appearing to be more likely, and TPVG is significantly underpriced if it can regain dividend coverage at the $0.30 level.

TPVG's dividend is higher risk. However, with earnings coming up TPVG is trading at a price with an intriguing risk/reward. If the bad scenario comes in, we are fine with paying current prices. If the good scenario comes in, we are thrilled to pay current prices.

Conclusion

TPVG has had a rough last couple of years. When your portfolio is designed to grow in size and you're forced to pull back on the reins, slowing your growth, or even allow your portfolio to reduce in size and leverage, your ability to pay out to your shareholders shrinks. As a BDC, TPVG is required to pay out 90% of its taxable earnings and gains, meaning that it's compelled to pay out the vast majority of what it brings in. This leaves very little room for them to be able to grow their portfolio by retaining capital. On the flip side, this means that if their portfolio shrinks in size and their taxable earnings decrease, the wise and prudent choice for them would be to reduce their rewards to shareholders, thereby avoiding unnecessary erosion of book value over time.

At this point in time, we'd like the yield available to TPVG if it isn't able to grow and has to reduce its dividend slightly. We also find it highly attractive if they're able to grow and cover their dividend even more securely. TPVG is not for the faint of heart at this point in time. An investor who is willing to take on some additional risk for a potentially larger reward could greatly benefit from owning shares in TPVG. However, if you were a more risk-averse investor, then there are other great opportunities that we recently covered, that would be a better fit for you.

data.title: The Bond King Has Spoken: 2 Near-Perfect 7-9%-Yielding Dividend Stocks For What's Next
data.description:
Introduction

Inflation is, by far, the most important topic I have discussed this year.

Generally speaking, we have spent a lot of time on this subject since the infamous price surge of 2021. Although inflation has decreased significantly since then, we're still facing above-average inflation, a Federal Reserve unwilling (read: unable) to cut rates, and the risks of a new wave of inflation, which supports my "higher for longer" thesis.

On July 20, for example, I wrote an article titled "Attention Dividend Investors: The Macro Shift You've Been Waiting For Is Here." Below is a part of my takeaway:

The macro tide is turning, which is why I'm doubling down on my "higher for longer" inflation (and rates) thesis.

Despite recent disinflation, core prices are creeping up to 2.9%, driven by rising goods and sticky services.

This isn't the pre-2021 low-rate world, as secular tailwinds are gone, and the Trump administration's push for nominal growth risks is further supporting higher inflation.

Moreover, energy prices, low inventories, and manufacturing momentum signal a cyclical upswing, similar to the early 2000s.

To use the words of Bloomberg, this is "A Move in the Wrong Direction."

Now, it seems my case is getting support from Bill Gross, the billionaire investor who co-founded PIMCO, one of the biggest fixed-income managers on the planet.

He managed the $270 billion Total Return Fund, which got him the "Bond King" nickname, which means when he speaks or writes, people tend to care.

Earlier this month, he published an article in the Financial Times titled "'Fire' is now the greater threat to investors than 'ice.'" The title was based on the words of Robert Frost, who used fire and ice to discuss desire and hate, respectively.

Mr. Gross used it as a placeholder for inflation and deflation when he brought up that modern finance is essentially a constant battle to avoid turmoil:

My point from this history is that our finance-based economy has been in a long-term fight to produce stability in the midst of destructive forces, some brought about by Keynesian attempts by governments to stimulate demand, others more influenced by the fragility of credit creation first explained by Hyman Minsky in the early 1980s. - Financial Times

Now, despite situations like the one in China, where the government seems to be unable to use stimulus to boost inflation and growth, Bill Gross makes the case that financial markets are more likely to see inflationary "singe" than a deflationary "freeze" in the future, which aligns with my own thesis.

Essentially, the article makes the case that the combination of finance-based capitalism and government neglect of the inflationary consequences of rising debt levels strongly biases the long-term outlook toward "fire."

So, what does this mean?

This implies that financial markets should prepare for increasing interest rates as a counter to potential productivity boosts from advancements like artificial intelligence, which adds another layer of complexity.

It also tends to make government bonds horrible investments.

That's not coming from me but from the Bond King himself:

Even with the 30-year yield at 4.9 per cent — some 0.9 percentage points above its 12-month lows — long-term bonds remain risky because of the high sensitivity of their prices to small changes in interest rates. A 0.2 percentage point rise in yields — which pushes Treasury prices down — would wipe out the annual return an investor can expect to make from coupon payments. That’s not a bonfire but the heat is above normal. - Financial Times

Interestingly, I never really liked government bonds anyway. That's not because of my macro view but because I prefer equities over loans. I want to own a piece of a great business. Potentially "forever."

Especially now, it makes sense to invest in high-quality income stocks, which is what the second part of this article is all about.

So, What Should We Be Buying?

Discussing "Big Picture" developments is important.

However, it's just a part of the deal, as finding suitable investments is just as important as having the right view on macroeconomics and geopolitics.

Just like in football, having a portfolio of great plays is one thing. Executing them on the field on Sunday is what matters most.

Luckily, Bill Gross also provided a solution for that.

In response to many questions, he went on X (formerly known as Twitter) to share his thoughts on the best places to put one's money. Needless to say, I won't be sharing these if I do not fully agree with them. Otherwise, what's the value of me if I simply tell you what others think? That wouldn't help my credibility.

Anyway, here's what he wrote:

For readers looking for specific recommendations, I continue to like MLP pipelines with their high tax-deferred dividends (7–9%) and future infrastructure prospects due to AI, AI information centers, electricity demand, and the natural gas needed to generate it. Plains All American (PAA), Western Midstream Partners (WES) are my favorites. Becoming intrigued with food stocks. They’re going nowhere pricewise but a 6.2% yield on KHC (Kraft Heinz) is attractive for income. It’s breaking the company into two parts which may push price a little higher. - Bill Gross on X

Readers who have been with me for a while know that I wholeheartedly agree with his assessment of midstream companies. These are the companies that own the pipelines and processing assets (some even own export facilities and other major assets) used by oil and gas companies to process and ship the products they extract from the ground.

These companies are not directly prone to oil and gas price changes, as most of their income comes from long-term contracts. This provides a thick layer of safety.

Additionally, due to a sharp decline in new projects in recent years, most midstream companies enjoy improved pricing power, reduced capital expenditure, which increases free cash flow, and the advantages of long-term secular growth driven by AI-powered energy demand (and other benefits).

I also have to say I'm glad Mr. Gross brought up PAA and WES, which are two Master Limited Partnerships I'm bullish on that I have not given the attention they deserve.

Please note that these companies issue K-1 forms. It also means their dividends are called "distributions" and their shares are called "units."

Some readers absolutely love MLPs due to tax advantages that prevent double income taxation, while others dislike them. Reasons to dislike them are tax complexities (for some), while others like me (non-American investors) cannot invest in MLPs unless it's through ETFs.

That's why I decided to go with Plains GP Holdings, which is the C-Corp variant that issues a 1099 form.

Now, let's take a closer look at these two companies, which both yield 8% and come with a lot more benefits than the quote from Bill Gross may suggest.

Plains GP Holdings (PAGP) - An "All-American" Income Solution

Let's start with some basics.

PAA (Plains All American Pipeline, L.P.) and PAGP (Plains GP Holdings, L.P.) are both publicly traded entities within the same energy infrastructure and logistics company.

However, they differ in their structure and tax implications.

  • PAA is a Master Limited Partnership and issues a K-1 tax form.
  • PAGP is structured as an "Up-C" and issues a 1099 form.

More importantly, despite these differences, both offer investors exposure to the same underlying economics and distributions from Plains All American's operations.

Plains recently spun off its natural gas liquids business to the Canadian Keyera Corporation in a deal worth $3.75 billion, which makes it a pure-play crude oil company.

The company is a heavyweight in key areas, including the Permian Basin, where it's looking to add close to 300,000 barrels per day in capacity this year, with a total crude oil EBITDA of $2.4 billion, way more than the $450 million in annual EBITDA it generated in its natural gas segment.

It has a total long-haul volume of 1.7 million barrels in the Permian, which accounts for more than half of its EBITDA. In this area, a quarter of its capacity is uncontracted, which leaves room for further output growth, even if we can assume that Permian output growth will be very slow at sub-$80 WTI.

According to the company, the recent oil price volatility has led producers to adopt a "wait and see" approach, with no immediate changes to activity levels expected in the two quarters.

I have heard the same from a wide range of other companies who expect volumes to remain unchanged in a best-case scenario, with a base-case scenario being a slight decline due to the elevated decline rates of American onshore oil producers.

The company expects that Permian long-haul pipelines will reach roughly 80% utilization in the coming years, driven by production growth and demand recovery. According to the company, its integrated asset base and strategic acquisitions position it to capitalize on this trend, with no immediate need for new long-haul pipelines unless production significantly exceeds expectations.

With that said, I also cannot make the case that Plains is a huge winner from AI. In that area, the biggest winners are natural gas pipeline owners.

However, general oil demand and supply are expected to remain strong, as I discussed in a lot of articles, including this one.

It also helps that the company almost exclusively deals with investment-grade counterparties, with roughly 80% contracted cash flows and an average contract term of five years. Just 20% of its income is dependent on commodity prices.

In general, despite flattening oil production growth since the pandemic, the company has grown its EBITDA every single year since at least 2021, with a four-year CAGR of 7%. During this period, it lowered its leverage ratio to its target range of 3.25-3.75x while hiking its dividend by 21% per year.

Currently yielding 7.6% (8% for simplicity, and because PAA yields north of 8.0%), the company aims to grow its distribution/dividend by $0.15 per unit/share in 2026, which would imply roughly 10% growth with a targeted coverage of 160%, a great number.

Moreover, its natural gas liquids divestiture is expected to result in an elevated special dividend (for tax purposes), which does not necessarily generate shareholder value. However, as a part of this, proceeds will be used for bolt-on acquisitions, and we can assume the streamlined crude oil giant will be able to potentially boost growth for years to come.

While I disagree with AI being a great tailwind for PAA/PAPG, I absolutely agree with Bill Gross when it comes to the increased attractiveness of this high-yielding midstream giant in light of inflation and bond risks.

Spoiler alert: the same goes for the second pick.

Western Midstream Partners (WES) - If I Could Buy MLPs, I Would Own It For Sure

Unlike most major midstream companies, Western Midstream is a company tied to a major producer. In this case, the company is owned by Occidental Petroleum (OXY), which owns 45% of the company and 2% of the operating entity.

Western Midstream was initially formed by Anadarko Petroleum to handle its midstream assets. In 2019, Occidental bought Anadarko. As part of debt reduction efforts, Occidental has sold parts of its Western Midstream stake, which explains why it owns slightly less than half.

It also made the company more independent.

Nonetheless, a good connection with Occidental is a benefit, as it creates visibility for WES when it comes to its operations and expansion opportunities.

Personally, I own Antero Midstream (AM), which has an even tighter relationship with Antero Resources (AR), something I like a lot.

With that said, unlike Plains, Western Midstream is much less heavy in oil.

As we can see below, the company is expected to generate up to $2.55 billion in adjusted EBITDA this year. More than half of this is expected to come from the Delaware Basin, a sub-basin of the Permian Basin. In this area, it's focused on gas. That's fantastic, as the entire Permian lacks natural gas takeaway capacity. The basin also has too much water, as some areas come with 10 barrels of produced (toxic) water per barrel of oil, which needs to be handled.

In the Delaware Basin, it makes as much money from water as from oil. Meanwhile, in the DJ Basin (the basin close to Denver), it generates almost all of its EBITDA from gas operations.

Combining all assets, it owns 21 gathering systems, 77 processing and treating facilities, 7 natural gas pipelines, and 11 crude oil/natural gas liquids pipelines. These cover more than 14,000 miles, with 95% of all natural gas contracts being fee-based. All of its liquids are shipped under fixed-fee deals.

With regard to its financials, the company has significantly strengthened its financial position since early 2020 through balance sheet optimization, asset portfolio streamlining, cost reductions, and substantial free cash flow generation.

As a result, the company now has its strongest balance sheet ever with an investment-grade credit rating, a net leverage ratio of less than 3x, and roughly $2.4 billion in liquidity, supporting organic growth, M&A, and shareholder distributions, as its capital allocation priorities below show.

It's also upbeat when it comes to growing the business, as it expects mid-single-digit percentage growth in natural gas and produced water throughput for 2025, with low single-digit growth for crude oil and NGLs. This excludes volumes from non-core asset sales that closed in early 2024.

Also, as one may have expected, the Delaware Basin remains the core driver of growth as the basin is home to America's deepest reserves, rising gas production, and a lot of produced water handling demand.

Even better, the company is generating free cash flow, with 65% of its capex being directed towards expansion programs. In other words, over time, the company will be in a great spot to further accelerate free cash flow as past investments pay off and growth capex declines.

For 2025, it expects roughly $1.4 billion in free cash flow, which translates to 9% of its market cap. This fully covers its 9% dividend and explains why the company targets long-term mid-to-single-digit annual distribution growth.

In fact, its yield clearly makes it a standout, as the chart below shows.

However, while one might make the case that the higher the yield (I generally agree with that), Western Midstream is a hidden gem to me as it has the right assets for America's energy future, including natural gas in the Permian, a booming water business, and a very healthy balance sheet.

Hence, as I said, if I could, I would buy it.

Takeaway

Inflation isn't going anywhere, at least not down.

To me, it's higher for longer.

And apparently, I'm not the only one with this view, as even the "Bond King" Bill Gross agrees.

With core prices creeping up, energy risks looming, and rates stuck in neutral, the old playbook seems to be dead.

That's why assets like government bonds continue to be very tricky for long-term investors.

The real opportunity lies in high-quality income stocks, especially in midstream energy.

Companies like Plains and Western Midstream offer >8% yields, inflation-resistant cash flows, and secular tailwinds from AI-driven power demand, the booming water business, limited natural gas egress in the Permian, and more.

Hence, I truly believe they make ideal hedges in this macro shift.

Risks To My Thesis

The risks to my thesis are very low, which makes for a great risk/reward.

Even if I'm wrong and inflation implodes (which would be great), these companies still own mission-critical assets with secular tailwinds. The biggest risk is a steep recession, which could cause investors to expect a decline in oil and gas volumes. However, because of fixed-fee contracts, these risks are subdued for both PAGP/PAA and WES.

data.title: U.K. PMI Sets Up A Divisive August Bank Of England Decision
data.description:
By James Smith

The latest UK Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) perfectly encapsulates the headache the Bank of England faces right now.

Higher payroll taxes and a chunky rise in the National Living Wage back in April are exerting more significant downward pressure on staffing numbers, according to the latest PMI. That helped lower the overall services index from 52.8 to 51.2 in July, and is in line with what the BoE’s own “decision maker” survey has been showing too. Remember, the official payroll figures have also consistently fallen this year, albeit fairly gradually so far.

But the PMI also suggests these policy changes are keeping upward pressure on prices. We’ve seen hints of this in the CPI data, principally in food, where inflation rates have picked up over and above what we’ve seen in the eurozone. Pressure on staffing costs also likely explains the stickiness in hospitality inflation through the spring.

Which of these trends – higher inflation or lower hiring – matters more? This is not a new debate and one that's far from resolved, setting up another divisive August Bank of England meeting. We wouldn't be at all surprised to see another three-way vote split, akin to what we saw in May. Back then, officials narrowly voted for a 25bp rate cut, but with two officials voting for no change, and a further two preferring a 50bp cut.

In our view, the Bank should be more concerned about the ominous state of the jobs market and what it implies for wage growth. We suspect private sector pay growth will inch closer to 4% (from 5% now) as the year progresses. Services inflation should come a little lower through the second half of the year too, albeit the concentration of annual price hikes in April means we’re unlikely to see materially better news until next Spring.

And that's the key – this is a gradually evolving story and suggests that the central debate at the BoE on inflation is unlikely to fully resolve itself imminently. Not enough to convince the majority of officials to rally behind faster rate cuts, anyway.

A more worrisome run of jobs data over the summer could change that. But for now, we think rate cuts in August and November are the path of least resistance, and the bar to moving faster remains set fairly high.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Tesla Is Building A Fully Automated World; Why One Weak Quarter Won't Stop It
data.description:
Holding Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) shares can be frustrating. While the stock generated alpha returns since its IPO, growing into a mega-cap Tech company, its performance in the past five years has been stagnant. Tesla trades close to its local 2020 all-time high. Q2 2025 earnings were somewhat disappointing, with the stock correcting after Musk’s comments about upcoming weak quarters.

I think Elon Musk is unapologetically focused on his long-term vision for the world and the role Tesla should play in it. Tesla’s CEO has been vocal about the fact he does not care about quarterly results. This, together with Musk’s recent involvement in politics, has made for some disappointing quarterly earnings.

Yet, I think Tesla Q2 2025 earnings also show signs of what’s to come; a peek into Musk’s vision for the world. This is what I will cover in this article.

Understanding Musk’s Vision Of A Fully Automated World

To understand Tesla’s future, one must first understand Musk’s vision for the future, which goes beyond Tesla. I think Musk envisions a world of full automation, which I would describe and classify in the following four categories:

  • Robotics will automate the workforce (and labor market), whether via AI agents (evolving from current large language models) or physical robots of different types, taking over most jobs from humans. The few humans left in the workforce will mostly have to do with interacting with and perfecting automation.

  • Energy will be abundant and predominantly relying on solar and batteries for storage. AI will be optimizing energy distribution to autonomous vehicles, homes, and factories. In this world, energy costs plummet, making mobility and automation cheaper. Consider that stars are the only source of energy at a cosmic and planetary level.

  • Mobility will change, and most people won’t drive cars, or even own vehicles at all. Rather, people will be driven by self-driving vehicles that they either rent short- or long-term. Driving and owning cars will be limited to collectors. Consider that car “ownership” intended as fully owning a car, is already a thing of the past. Most consumers today prefer to lease, finance, or rent. This “access over ownership” model may also expand beyond cars. People may subscribe to services for housing, food production, and even education (delivered by AI tutors). Once again, this is a trend that is already taking place with the rise of subscription service models.

  • Connectivity will be widespread, in line with energy being abundant and cheap. Musk’s Starlink could, for example, blanket the planet with high-speed internet itself, enabling real-time data sharing for autonomous systems. Ultimately, an automated world can only be highly connected one.

  • Human augmentation and cognitive enhancement will be another key area shaping the future. Brain-computer interfaces may enhance human capabilities, enabling seamless interaction with AI and automation, augmenting cognition, and addressing neurological conditions. This may seem sci-fi today (and it is, to some extent). However, readers may want to consider advanced reality glasses like those of Apple Inc. (AAPL) or Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) are by themselves the first steps towards integrating the physical and digital worlds.

To make this vision happen, I see two enabling factors.

The first enabler is software. Software enables any sort of automation. Software is key for anything from factory robots to humanoid robots and self-driving cars (which, effectively, could be seen as robots on wheels). But software is also key for relatively more mundane tasks, such as running Musk’s Starlink satellite network for global collectivity, or optimizing the way solar panels activate depending on weather and season. Software is already key in our lives today - it runs our monetary system, day work, and more. It is set to become even more central as the world goes towards automation.

When I say “software” I mostly mean advanced AI models that can autonomously operate and seamlessly interface with humans and machines. Here is where xAI, Musk’s own AI company, comes in. xAI was founded only 2 years ago, and it is now

The second enabler is scalable, low-cost industrial production. A fully automated world can only exist if robots - be it on wheels, on foot, flying, floating, or stationary - are available in mass. The same applies to any other underlying product that allows robotics automation - from solar panels and batteries to satellites, computers, GPUs, etc.

What Is Tesla’s Role?

Readers may have noticed how I mentioned a few of Musk’s companies in my previous section. I think Musk is building an automation ecosystem where every single one of his companies plays a dedicated role. Tesla, in my view, sits at the core of this ecosystem.

The image above is my visualization of Musk’s ecosystem and Tesla’s role in it. Note that I excluded SpaceX, as it is irrelevant for the scope of this article, and only included X (formerly Twitter) in its role to train AI LLMs (Robotics), not as a social media platform.

I see Tesla as the only company of Musk that spans between vastly different categories of the world Musk is building. Tesla is going to be a key player in Mobility (where it already is), Energy (a segment growing in importance), and Robotics (still in its relatively early stages). More of my thoughts about these segments will follow in the next sections, focused on Q2 earnings.

On top of being a player spanning different categories, I think Tesla also plays a disproportionate role in both enablers of a fully automated world: software and industrial production.

When it comes to software, Tesla’s ability to capture real-world data from its cars is crucial. For example, the principles of computer vision, sensor fusion, and neural network training developed for Tesla's Full Self-Driving systems are transferable to robotics, such as the Tesla Optimus humanoid robot.

Tesla’s proprietary data feeds into automation algorithms, improving them over time. Together with user data from X (formerly Twitter), this can also be used in wider robotics applications, especially when it comes to areas like perception, navigation, and decision-making.

Industrial production is perhaps an even clearer example of Tesla’s edge in this world. The company has been built to standardize car manufacturing to the extreme in order to achieve scalability. To the point that it had to partially revert some of its decisions, such as the removal of the turn signal stalk from its Model 3 car.

The fact that Tesla started with manufacturing cars is in itself not a coincidence, in my opinion. Elon Musk himself has mentioned how manufacturing Electric Vehicles is “insanely difficult”. There may be a design behind this decision: mastering car manufacturing makes it doable to jump to manufacturing robots, which Tesla already allegedly does (in small numbers), having even put some of them to work on the factory floor.

All in all, if one is to believe Musk’s vision of the world (a risk in itself, as I will outline later), investing in Tesla means investing is the key player of a fully automated world.

What Q2 Tesla Earnings Tell Me About Tesla’s Mission

With such a long-term vision, it is difficult to reconcile Tesla’s wide ambitions with its quarterly results. Yet, I think there are signs of Tesla’s future in each quarterly earnings call, with Q2 2025 not being an exception.

Tesla’s shares are down approximately 5% in pre-market (at the time of writing). However, they were flat or even slightly up immediately after earnings yesterday evening. The stock corrected only after Elon Musk’s comments during the call that followed. Musk mentioned that he expects two to three weak quarters in the near future, due to policy changes, tariffs, and reduced regulatory credits. This, in my view, is a sign that what “scared” the market are not Q2 financials by themselves, but rather this forward guidance.

At face value, Q2 financials were not great for a company that currently capitalizes more than $1 trillion and trades at a rich valuation, with a forward P/E ratio above 150. The table below highlights key financial figures I find relevant.

I find particularly worrying the decline in the Energy segment, which went from growing more than 60% Y-o-Y in Q1 to declining by 7%. The reason, called out in the earnings deck, is lower Average Selling Price [ASP]. I believe prices were reduced to accelerate further battery adoption, following what Tesla has done historically for its automotive segment. Additionally, tariffs added $ 300 million in sequential costs, with "approximately 2/3 of that impact in automotive and rest in energy”. I would have liked to see Musk providing more insights on Energy, other than mentioning record Powerwall deployments.

Another element worth noting is Free Cash Flow, which declined by 89%. This, in my view, is a result of both the impact of tariffs as well as the increase in Capex for AI - which I will cover shortly. I don’t see this as negative news per se, but rather a sign the company is heavily investing for the long run, a good thing in my view.

On the positive side, Tesla registered a slight increase in margins (which went up to 4.1% from 2.1% in Q1) and saw its Services segment growing by a healthy 17% Y-o-Y.

Beyond quarterly financials, I think there are further signs that Tesla is focused on executing their long-term vision of a fully automated world. These are the elements from Q2 earnings that I see as key to the long-term thesis:

  1. Musk discussed progress toward Optimus 3, with prototypes expected by year-end and scale production targeted for 2026, aiming for 1 million units annually within five years.

  2. Tesla expanded AI training computing with an additional 16k H200 GPUs at Gigafactory Texas, bringing the total to 67k H100 GPUs equivalent.

  3. Tesla plans a gradual robotaxi rollout, allowing third-party vehicles in 2026, with funding shifting to debt once revenues stabilize.

I see these announcements as fitting the idea that Tesla is focusing on the two enablers of Musk’s vision: software and mass industrial production.

For what concerns software, building massive capacity of GPUs is a need at this point in time. I think we are in an AI race, and Tesla cannot stay out and simply rely on xAI or other companies in Musk’s portfolio (which legally Tesla has no significant ties to). This is why I do not see the Q2 FCF decline as an issue, even if it does represent a potential headwind for the company, something I will cover in my risk section.

From an industrial production standpoint, I am reassured by Musk’s comments on Optimus 3 progress and the idea that Tesla should be able to start scaling production as early as 2026. I see this as comparable to a new “Model 3 moment,” where Tesla showed it could mass produce vehicles when it first launched its Model 3 car.

My Thoughts About Tesla’s Valuation And A 10x From Here

In one of my past articles on Tesla, I covered what the company would look like if it were to grow into (and beyond) its current market valuation. Whether or not this will happen, the market is betting there is at least a chance it does, as it assigns a rich valuation to Tesla.

With that in mind, I find it pointless to run valuation models or provide short-term price targets. I think Tesla has the potential of reaching at the very least a $2 trillion valuation (representing a 2X on current prices). I do not think it’s unlikely Tesla can eventually reach $10 trillion in market capitalization in the long run (a 10X on current prices), if it manages to become one of the key players of a fully automated world.

The table below, taken from my previous work, outlines what a $10 trillion Tesla would look like, assuming a long-term terminal P/E of 35 and a net profit margin (net income) of ~25% company-wise, in line with that of Apple today. I invite readers who are interested in more of my thoughts on Tesla’s valuation to read my previous work.

Readers who believe Tesla should trade at a car industry standard of around 10X to 20X P/E ratio should, in my view, simply avoid Tesla and buy any traditional car manufacturer instead. An investment in Tesla only makes sense if one is to believe the long-term thesis and is willing to look beyond quarterly data.

Risks Of Investing In Tesla Today

Tesla is focused on executing Musk’s long-term vision for the world. In this context, the company finds itself heavily investing in GPUs (to develop its AI) and doing aggressive price cuts to put more cars on the road and batteries in the market. The more cars and batteries, the more data and the better Tesla’s software capabilities get.

The underlying risk in this scenario is that Tesla, unlike other Tech companies, has to deal with relatively low margin, Capex-intensive operations. As a car manufacturer, it does not enjoy the same margins and FCF of companies that deal with online Advertising or Cloud services. Nor does it enjoy the same margins of Apple or NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA). Manufacturing cars and batteries is simply not as profitable as selling Online Ads.

While I think Tesla is far better positioned long term than asset-light Tech companies (as it is effectively the only one with industrial capabilities), this dynamic is sure to put pressure on Tesla’s financials for the foreseeable future. The company will be under pressure for what concerns their margins and FCF. It may incur in issuing more debt or even diluting shareholders to finance itself. While shares outstanding have been stabilizing in the past couple of years, historically Tesla does dilute its shareholders.

Other risks with an investment in Tesla involve delays with Musk’s execution plan (which have already happened in the past) and a “Musk Premium” which may cause uncertainty and volatility. The stock has been very volatile in the past six months, a result of Musk’s political involvement, headwinds in the automotive industry, and other macro factors.

While I believe all bad news has been priced in at this point, I may very well be wrong. Investors should only consider entering Tesla if they plan to hold for the long term and are comfortable with volatility.

Conclusion

Tesla is the only major US tech company that is not only a leader in software but also owns its industrial production base. Unlike Apple or Nvidia, which rely on third-party factories, Tesla owns and operates its production base. While Apple and Nvidia hold intellectual property rights for their specialized products, their manufacturing processes are less scalable compared to Tesla’s unique, vertically integrated approach. In other words, Apple was never able to expand from smartphones to cars, while Tesla is expanding from cars to robots and batteries.

This is both Tesla’s greatest advantage and its greatest tailwind. As a capex-heavy company that (for now) finances itself mostly by selling cars and batteries, Tesla is set to experience more headwinds from interest rates, policy, and other macro factors. It needs to heavily invest in AI by scaling up GPU capabilities, but it does not have the same margins and FCF as companies selling Online Ads or Cloud services.

However, in my view, Tesla is also the only Tech company to have what it takes to deliver on an all-encompassing mission of fully automating the world. Together with other companies in Musk’s “ecosystem”, Tesla’s vision spans mobility, energy, robotics, and more. It is simultaneously investing in industrial production capabilities, AI models, new battery technology, cars, robots, robotaxis, and more.

Personally, I won’t sit out on Tesla’s mission to fully automate the world due to a few bad quarters. This is a long-term, relatively high-risk bet on a company that has the potential to grow into a $10 trillion valuation. It is not for all, and investors who are cautious about volatility should better steer clear of Tesla. For investors that have a long-term view, I think an allocation to Tesla is almost a need, as a bet on a fully automated future. While it is not a short-term bet, it is an asymmetric investment nonetheless. That’s why I rate Tesla a BUY.

data.title: XMHQ: The Recent Portfolio Rebalance Fuels Price Returns, But Still Appears Unattractive
data.description:
Invesco S&P MidCap Quality ETF's (NYSEARCA:XMHQ) recent portfolio rebalance has increased its ability to deliver the higher share price and dividend returns. The majority of its top holdings are among the fast-growing companies. Consequently, since the rebalance, XMHQ has begun to outperform its peers. The fund is also likely to benefit from the broader mid-cap category rally on hopes over rate cuts. Therefore, I upgrade my rating to buy from sell.

XMHQ Portfolio Rebalance Changed the Course

In my last article, I argued that Invesco S&P MidCap Quality ETF's portfolio structure is composed of numerous underperforming stocks, which are at risk of performing poorly ahead. The fund underperformed in the first half of the year due to the sluggish portfolio holdings performance.

However, the recent portfolio rebalance has positioned XMHQ's portfolio to capitalize on the potential uptrend in the mid-cap category on hopes over rate cuts.

XMHQ slashed its stake in the struggling technology, energy, and basic material stocks while increasing its exposure to the industrials, financial services, consumer discretionary, and health care sectors.

With this change in the portfolio structure, XMHQ's share price began rebounding. In the last month, its price rebound was the highest in its peer group.

XMHQ exited its stake in Williams-Sonoma (WSM), which was its largest holding with a weight above 5%. Year to date, Williams-Sonoma share price is struggling to trade in a green.

The fund also exited or lowered its stakes in other top holdings, including Pure Storage (PSTG), Duolingo (DUOL), and RenaissanceRe Holdings (RNR). Similarly, it also made several changes in the rest of its portfolio.

The portfolio changes in XMHQ and its benchmark index are based on the quality factors, including the return on equity, financial leverage, and accruals ratio. The fund rebalances its portfolio semi-annually.

After the change, Carlisle Companies (CSL), EMCOR Group (EME), Comfort Systems (FIX), Manhattan Associates (MANH), and Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM) are its top 5 holdings.

XMHQ increased its stake in Carlisle from 3.5% to nearly 5%. It appears that the fund's strategy is working because the shares of Carlisle Companies soared nearly 16% year to date. Carlisle belongs to the industrials sector, operating in the building products industry. The rally in its price is supported by investors optimism over the potential policy easing in the second half and ahead.

The fund also increased its stake in construction and engineering companies, including EMCOR Group and Comfort Systems.

EMCOR Group earned a strong buy rating based on the Seeking Alpha quant system. Year to date, its share price rallied at a blistering pace of 26%. The share price of Comfort Systems is also up 28% year to date.

Manhattan Associates also begins to impress investors through its solid financial growth. The company topped the second quarter expectations and lifted its full-year outlook.

Its June quarter adjusted earnings per share increased 11% year over year to $1.31. The company lifted its full-year revenue outlook in the range of $1.071 billion and $1.075 billion, up from the recent range of $1.060 billion to $1.070 billion. Manhattan Associates now expects adjusted earnings per share in the range of $4.76 to $4.84 compared to the previous expectations of $4.54 to $4.64.

Sprouts Farmers Market is a newcomer in its top 10 holdings. The company's stock price soared 29% year to date amid its robust financial growth potential. The company's earnings are expected to increase by 35% in 2025.

The health care stocks, including United Therapeutics (UTHR), Medpace Holdings (MEDP), and Exelixis (EXEL), are also the new members of its top 10 holdings. Shares of Medpace Holdings and Exelixis are soaring at a solid pace. Exelixis also earned a strong buy rating, according to the Seeking Alpha quant system.

Overall, the fund's strategy of exiting or lowering its stake in struggling companies while increasing its exposure to fast-growing companies from the industrials, financials services, health care, and consumer discretionary sectors positions it to deliver solid price returns.

XMHQ Quant Rating and Other Characteristics

Although XMHQ's quant rating improved significantly in the past six months, it still earned a hold rating with a quant score of 2.58.

I believe XMHQ's quant score is likely to increase in the coming months amid the potential improvement in the momentum and risk factors. This is because the fund's portfolio rebalance increased its stake in fast-growing companies, which are expected to fuel its share price performance in the coming months while lowering the volatility level.

XMHQ's expense ratio of 0.25% is lower than the median of all ETFs. In addition, its liquidity appears strong, with $5 billion in assets under management and 256K in average share trading volume.

XMHQ also appears attractively priced given its cheap valuations. Its shares are currently trading around 19x trailing and 18x forward earnings.

Its peers, such as Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF Shares (VOT), Invesco S&P MidCap Momentum ETF (XMMO) and Vanguard Mid-Cap Index Fund ETF Shares (VO), are trading around trailing P/E ratio of 36.5, 21 and 23, respectively.

Risk Factors to Consider

There are multiple risk factors to consider when it comes to investing in the mid-cap category.

The mid caps are sensitive to monetary and economic conditions. In the case of lower than expected cuts and economic uncertainty, the mid caps can underperform. In particular, XMHQ's exposure to industrial companies increases its dependence on the interest rates and economic stability. Furthermore, the fund can also respond to the broader stock market selloffs.

Final Thoughts

As optimism in the risky stocks continue to grow amid hopes over rate cuts and economic stability, investing the in the mid-cap category could be a smart strategy. This is because mid caps have the history of generating market-beating returns in easing conditions. Invesco S&P MidCap Quality ETF's appears to be worth considering investment option due to its portfolio rebalance. XMHQ's portfolio now offers healthy capital appreciation with a moderate risk factor.

data.title: Upwork Looks Like A Good (Investing) Gig
data.description:
While I haven’t really been on the platform much these past few years, Upwork (and Elance before it) was once a primary source of income. As with many of these platforms, there were goods and bads, but on the whole, I appreciated the opportunities afforded. Looking at Upwork now, I think the company offers a solid investment opportunity and favorable valuation.

Still, there are some key threats that could undermine Upwork (UPWK), including artificial intelligence potentially limiting the gig economy. Meanwhile, a tight labor market may reduce the number and/or quality of freelancers. Further, with many businesses adopting a wait-and-see approach owing to tariffs and economic uncertainty, budgets for freelancers may be under pressure. Still, these concerns are speculative and likely not near-term threats. Given the overall strength of the gig economy, I believe Upwork has bright days ahead.

A Gig Here, A Gig There, A Gig Everywhere

Upwork ranks among the largest freelancing platforms in the world, alongside Freelancer and Fiverr (FVRR). Clients can find freelancers offering a plethora of different services, including data analysis, programming, content creation, and more. Freelancers, too, can find many opportunities, and parties the world over can get in touch and collaborate with one another. In the United States, an estimated 64 million people freelanced in 2023, and by 2028, freelancers in the USA alone (some part-time, others full-time). In other words, the gig economy is massive, and outside of transportation, Upwork holds a dominant market position.​​

Looking at the infographic above, you’ll note that freelancers are projected to top 90 million by 2028. Why so much growth? Many businesses seem to prefer freelancers since they don’t have to pay for benefits like healthcare, and letting them go is often easier than laying off employees. Further, many freelancers may also prefer freelancing, as they can often work from home and on their own schedule (within reason, of course).

The data discussed so far has largely been in regard to the United States. However, Upwork supports both freelancers and businesses the world over. Upwork has enabled companies big and small to outsource tasks, thus freeing up employees to work on more challenging tasks. Upwork also helps companies fill in gaps. A local mom-and-pop coffee shop probably doesn’t have the need for a full-time marketing expert. However, hiring such an expert for a few hours here and a few hours there could pay off in a big way.

Yet with AI growing more popular, one does have to wonder if many workers will end up obsolete.

AI is an Opportunity and a Threat

I primarily used Upwork to land various writing jobs. Finance, healthcare, pets, I wrote content on pretty much everything. Upwork reports that full-time freelancers on their website pull in about $41 per hour. At least when I was working on the website, it was relatively easy to command more than $65 per hour. And for a long time, gigs were plentiful. I could log in, find at least 10 interesting gigs, then apply to them, and I typically could expect to land 2-4 of the projects.

Now-a-days? AI has been gobbling up many of the smaller projects and content writing jobs. While I’m not working on Upwork right now, I have taken a gander at the job postings from time to time. Not only are there far fewer writing jobs than in the past, but pay has been declining, and the number of applicants competing for various jobs can quickly reach into the dozens.

Copywriting isn’t dead. Companies still need copy. Some companies still need or want old-fashioned human copy. Yet human copywriting has started to trend more toward niche demand than a business staple. In many situations and for many (but not all) topics, AI can generate decent enough webpage copy, blogs, summary bullet points (say for a brochure), so on and so forth.

Circling back to Upwork, this means that the platform could see fewer and fewer writing jobs posted. Upwork gets a cut of projects, so fewer writing jobs will result in lower revenue. Writers may leave the platform or downgrade to unpaid subscription plans as well, further costing Upwork money. The loss of writing jobs in and of itself will probably produce limited damage to Upwork. Yet we’re also seeing AI stepping in to write code, optimize ad campaigns, handle customer service chats, and so on.

Still, it’s not all bad news. Website designers, programmers, content strategists, and many other professionals can leverage AI to improve efficiency and to provide better services. Businesses in need of such services can turn to Upwork’s large pool of freelancers. You can even find AI talent on the platform. At least for now, I don't believe AI is an existential threat to Upwork, although it probably is a serious threat for people in specific roles.

Why I Could Be Wrong

First, we’re starting to see some pushback against the gig economy, and it’s not just customers and gig workers either. Governments have, in some cases, become leery of freelancers and gig workers. Regulatory crackdowns and reclassifying freelancers as employees have affected the rideshare industry and other parts of the gig economy. I don’t think Upwork is as exposed, but there may still be regulatory risks.

I may also be underestimating AI. At the moment, I see it as a productivity booster in more fields. However, as the technology advances, more and more people may become “obsolete.” This could reduce demand for freelance labor.

UPWK Fundamentals and Valuation

First, Upwork’s P/E GAAP [TTM] weighs in at just 8.25, well below Fiverr’s 53.72. Given how high PE ratios have gotten in the United States, such a low PE ratio jumps off the page. Of course, sometimes low PE ratios hint at serious trouble, but in Upwork’s case, I am not finding anything that scares me off. Upwork has $622 million in cash and short-term investments versus $645.7 in total liabilities, while net income measures ~$235 million. For quite some time, Upwork struggled to become profitable, but over the last few years, profits have swollen.

Revenue growth is perhaps a tad bit slow at 7.21%. The net income margin [TTM] is an excellent 30.46%, while the gross profit margin comes in at 77.78%. Fiverr, meanwhile, has a net income margin of just 4.51%, but revenues are growing at 10.41%.

Turning to GuruFocus’s DCF calculator, I find UPWK may be dramatically undervalued. In order to justify current prices, Upwork will have to secure FCF growth of just 1.36%. Setting FCF growth at 20%, which is in line with UPWK’s levered FCF growth, I find UPWK’s fair value to be north of $38. Even if we cut FCF to 10%, UPWK is still worth more than $21, as illustrated above.

(I set the discount rate at 10%, as I use that as my standard rate for most companies to make them easier to compare. The terminal growth rate was set at 4% to align with typically observed GDP growth rates.)

My fellow Seeking Alpha analysts aren’t as bullish as me, and overall, UPWK is classified as a hold. Seeking Alpha’s quant tools, on the other hand, agree that Upwork is a buy. I’ll admit, I’d have liked to see stronger revenue growth, but beyond that, Upwork seems to be in good shape in terms of fundamentals and valuation.

Upwork will be releasing earnings on August 6. Currently, the consensus EPS estimate is for 26 cents, unchanged from a year prior. However, Upwork last reported earnings of 34 cents, well above the projected 25 cents. I suspect Upwork will beat estimates again this quarter and wouldn't be surprised if EPS was above 30 cents.

Key Takeaway: Upwork Looks Like a Good Gig to Me

Upwork’s favorable fundamentals and turn towards profitability earn a buy rating from me. There are serious risks, like AI and regulatory changes, to keep an eye on, but that’s pretty common these days in many different industries. I also expect demand for freelance labor to remain high for now, and cost-cutting could become more important. This may ultimately benefit Upwork and gig workers.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Tesla Touts Ambitious Plans, Market Shrugs
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Tesla (TSLA) targets autonomous ride-hailing for half of U.S. by year-end while ramping Optimus and energy initiatives. (00:26) Chipotle (CMG) signals confidence in return to mid-single-digit comps while expanding new restaurant openings. (02:02) Trump to visit Federal Reserve, escalating pressure on Chair Powell. (02:42)

This is an abridged transcript.

Tesla (TSLA) is down 6% in premarket action.

The company reported second quarter results that were not as bad as Wall Street feared.

It avoided a second consecutive top- and bottom-line miss with profits in-line with expectations.

Tesla (TSLA) executives, including CEO Elon Musk and CFO Vaibhav Taneja assured investors that the company has ambitious plans.

Those plans include its robotaxi service, full self-driving (FSD) and a more affordable electric vehicle. Tesla plans to deliver on this despite regulatory headwinds, production challenges, and negative impacts from both tariffs and provisions within the “Big Beautiful Bill.”

Because of the company’s focus on the robotaxi rollout in Austin last month, production release of autopilot is “several months behind.”

Following the launch in Austin, Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) is also seeking regulatory approval for robotaxis in the San Francisco Bay Area, Nevada, and Florida.

The CEO predicted that robotaxis will be available to “half the U.S. population by the end of this year.”

Tesla’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) goal of a more affordable EV will be achieved later this year as the company seeks to maximize production by the third quarter for delivery in Q4.

Elon Musk said losing EV incentives could result in “a few rough quarters”, but the goal of the company remains to make the most affordable car it can.

Tesla is also our top story in the Wall Street Breakfast newsletter, our daily one-page news summary. Sign up for the newsletter here.

Chipotle Mexican Grill (NYSE:CMG) is on our biggest movers list. CMG is down 10% premarket.

Shares fell after the company missed revenue estimates with its Q2 earnings report.

Total revenue rose 3.0% due to new store openings, but comparable sales fell 4.0%, worse than -2.9% expected during the quarter that ended on June 30. Transactions were down 4.9% during the quarter.

Chipotle now expects flat comparable restaurant sales growth for the full year.

The link to this story in show notes will be a full breakdown of CMG’s earnings call.

President Donald Trump will make a rare visit to the Federal Reserve, the first by a sitting U.S. president in nearly two decades.

The visit is scheduled for 4 p.m. ET today.

The move marks a symbolic challenge to the central bank’s independence, as Trump continues to criticize Chairman Jerome Powell for not cutting interest rates.

The President last week sought input from Republican lawmakers on the possibility of firing Powell. He later walked back that idea.

Key takeaways from Alphabet's Q2 beat

Servicenow targets $15B-plus subscription revenue for 2026 as AI momentum drives 21.5% growth

IBM raises 2025 free cash flow guidance above $13.5B while accelerating AI-driven portfolio

The FTSE 100 is up 1% and the DAX is up 0.8%.
